rationale: The cited paper is discussed in the hypothesis-development section as direct evidence that religious priming can activate a sense of divine support and reduce responses to threatening persuasive messages. The citing paper uses that finding to motivate its own predictions about how religious priming changes reactions after service failure, and it later revisits the citation when positioning its contribution on God salience effects in consumer behavior. This is more than a passing literature-list mention because the cited result informs the conceptual setup and interpretation of the new studies.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Wu and Cutright (2018) by transferring the broader God-salience logic from fear-appeal effectiveness to negative word of mouth after service failure. It extends that line with a different outcome domain and a fairness-violation/forgiveness process model.
